title: Gougères
subtitle: Savory choux cheese puffs from Burgundy
excerpt: Gougères are small savory choux puffs made with the same basic paste as profiteroles, with grated Gruyère beaten in before baking. They puff dramatically in the oven, developing a crisp shell and a soft, slightly hollow interior with pockets of melted cheese. Served warm at aperitif time throughout Burgundy. Takes 40 minutes from start to table.
sourceNotes: Gougères are a Burgundian speciality, documented in French cookery from at least the nineteenth century and traditionally served in wine cellars alongside tastings. The inclusion of cheese in the choux paste is the defining characteristic. Gruyère is traditional, but Comté or a well-aged Cheddar can substitute. The gougère appears in Auguste Escoffier's Le Guide Culinaire (1903) and in regional Burgundian cookbooks throughout the twentieth century.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the baked gougères. Reheat from frozen at 190 degrees C fan for 8 minutes until crisp and warm through.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Pipe the raw paste onto trays and freeze unbaked; bake from frozen, adding 5 minutes to the bake time.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 22 to 26 minutes until puffed, deep gold, and hollow-sounding when tapped; do not open the oven in the first 20 minutes

content.text: The method is identical to profiteroles up to the point where the eggs have been incorporated. At that stage, beat in the grated cheese and a pinch of black pepper and nutmeg. The paste is then piped into rounds and baked. Use milk as well as water in the paste if you want a slightly richer, more golden result. Gruyère is the traditional choice; a firm, aged cheddar works well as a substitute.

Heat the oven to 200 degrees C fan. Line two baking trays with baking paper. In a saucepan, bring 75 ml water, 75 ml milk, 60 g diced butter, and half a teaspoon of salt to a rolling boil. Remove from the heat, add 100 g sifted plain flour all at once, and beat until the paste pulls away from the pan. Return to a low heat for 1 minute to dry it slightly. Transfer to a bowl and cool for 5 minutes. Beat in the eggs gradually, stopping when the paste falls in a slow, heavy ribbon. Beat in 100 g finely grated Gruyère, a quarter teaspoon of black pepper, and a quarter teaspoon of nutmeg. Spoon or pipe walnut-sized rounds onto the trays, spacing well apart. Scatter a little extra cheese over each if you have it. Bake for 22 to 26 minutes until puffed, deep gold, and hollow-sounding when tapped. Serve warm.

items.problem: Gougères are dense rather than puffed
items.solution: The paste was either too wet (too much egg) or the oven was not hot enough. The paste should drop from a spoon in a slow, heavy ribbon. If the oven is too cool, the steam that causes the puff cannot form fast enough.

items.problem: The gougères collapsed after 10 minutes out of the oven
items.solution: They were underbaked. The outside must be completely set before they come out or the steam inside will simply soften the shell. Pierce each one with a skewer as it comes from the oven to allow steam to escape.
